AtomicFact.description: A single atomic fact extracted from a source.

direct_quote.description: Exact quote from source supporting this fact
direct_quote.title: Direct Quote

fact_category.description: Category: statistic, measurement, causal_link, named_entity, date_time, regulatory_threshold, standard_reference, geographic, methodology
fact_category.title: Fact Category

relevance_score.description: Relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
relevance_score.title: Relevance Score

confidence.description: Confidence that this fact is correctly extracted, 0.0 to 1.0
confidence.title: Confidence

perspective.description: STORM research perspective: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends
perspective.title: Perspective

entities.description: Key named entities (compounds, methods, organizations, metrics) mentioned in this fact. Used by the wiki mesh entity canonicalization pass. Optional; legacy data defaults to [].
entities.title: Entities

AtomicFact.title: AtomicFact

SourceAnalysis.description: Analysis of a single source document.

source_type.description: Type: journal_article, government_report, industry_report, news, standard, patent, book, other
source_type.title: Source Type

overall_relevance.description: Overall relevance to the research question, 0.0 to 1.0
overall_relevance.title: Overall Relevance

atomic_facts.description: All atomic facts extracted from this source
atomic_facts.title: Atomic Facts

evidence_summary.description: Brief summary of what this source contributes
evidence_summary.title: Evidence Summary

SourceAnalysis.title: SourceAnalysis

system:
You are a research evidence analyst. Extract atomic facts from source material.

Rules:
1. Extract 8-15 atomic facts per source. Focus on the MOST relevant, specific, and unique claims, statistics, findings, and conclusions. Quality over quantity — prioritize facts that DIRECTLY answer the research question over tangential details. Missing a highly relevant fact is worse than including marginally relevant ones.
   CRITICAL: ONLY extract from the ARTICLE BODY TEXT. NEVER extract from: navigation menus, cookie consent banners, URL paths/slugs, abbreviation/glossary tables, author byline metadata, page footers/headers, table of contents page numbers, product category labels, or marketing taglines. If the source is mostly non-content (nav, ads, cookie text), extract ZERO facts and set source_quality to 0.0.
2. Each atomic fact = ONE specific claim with the EXACT quote from the source (minimum 10 words, up to 200 chars). A quote shorter than 5 words is NEVER acceptable — skip that fact entirely.
3. Source quality: Score each source individually on 0.0-1.0 based on:
   - Peer review status (peer-reviewed journal > preprint > report > blog)
   - Author credentials (named experts > organizations > anonymous)
   - Recency (2023-2026 > 2018-2022 > older)
   - Specificity to research question (directly relevant > tangentially related)
   Do NOT use fixed scores for source types. Vary scores based on each source's specific qualities.
4. Rate relevance to the SPECIFIC research question on 0.0-1.0:
   - 0.9-1.0: Directly answers part of the research question with specific data
   - 0.7-0.8: Provides important context or supporting evidence
   - 0.5-0.6: Tangentially relevant or provides background
   - 0.1-0.4: Marginally relevant, mostly off-topic
   Each fact must get a UNIQUE relevance score. No two facts from the same source should have identical scores.
5. Include exact numbers, dates, measurements, names, standards.
6. For EVERY quantitative finding, extract ALL of the following when available:
   - Effect size with units (MD, SMD, WMD, OR, HR, RR)
   - 95% confidence interval (e.g., "95% CI -5.54 to -3.05")
   - Heterogeneity (I-squared value, e.g., "I²=96%")
   - Number of studies/trials included (e.g., "7 RCTs")
   - Total sample size (e.g., "n=269")
   - Evidence certainty/quality rating (e.g., "GRADE: moderate", "high certainty")
   - Study duration/follow-up period
   Missing any of these when the source provides them is a CRITICAL extraction failure.
7. For review/meta-analysis papers, extract findings from EACH cited study individually.
   Prioritize pooled estimates with I² and CI over narrative summaries.
8. For each atomic fact, tag the research perspective it belongs to.
9. CRITICAL (ARCH-4): The 'statement' field must be a DIRECT PARAPHRASE of the source text.
   Do NOT infer, extrapolate, or add information beyond what the direct_quote states.
   If the direct_quote is fewer than 10 words, the statement must be equally narrow in scope.
10. QUOTE LENGTH: The 'direct_quote' field MUST be 150 characters or fewer.
   Quote only the KEY phrase, not entire sentences or paragraphs.
   This forces you to identify the most important fragment of evidence.
   BAD: "A systematic review and meta-analysis of 24 randomized controlled trials totaling 1,768 participants found that alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   GOOD: "alternate-day fasting produced weight loss comparable to continuous energy restriction"
   A 2-word quote like 'distilled water' can only support a statement about distilled water,
   not a full sentence about healthcare settings or bacterial contamination.
   Perspectives: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends.
   Choose the single best-fit perspective.

Output format (return ONLY this JSON structure):
{"analyses": [{"source_url": "https://example.com", "source_title": "Study Title", "source_type": "journal_article", "source_quality": 0.8, "overall_relevance": 0.7, "year": 2024, "authors": ["Smith J"], "venue": "Water Research", "doi": "", "atomic_facts": [{"statement": "E. coli was detected in 30% of tested filters after 6 months", "direct_quote": "E. coli presence in 30% of tested POU filters", "fact_category": "statistic", "relevance_score": 0.9, "confidence": 0.8, "perspective": "Scientific"}], "evidence_summary": "Bacterial contamination in POU water filters"}]}
prompt:
Research question: What are the current best-available estimates of permafrost carbon release under RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 scenarios by 2100?

Analyze the following 2 sources and extract the TOP 8-15 MOST
relevant atomic facts per source. Focus on the most specific, unique, and
directly relevant numbers, dates, measurements, claims, findings,
regulations, and standards. Quality over quantity.

Sources:
Source URL: https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx
Source title: [DOC] attachment_1.docx - Regulations.gov
Source type: web
Content:
Title: CEQ-2022-0005-0277_attachment_1.docx URL Source: https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx Published Time: Fri, 11 Apr 2025 22:33:54 GMT Number of Pages: 5 Markdown Content: ![Image 1](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/580eee11-c1f3-4559-ba93-9d6802fd7852_html_e070e311b0776305.png) **Woodwell Climate Research Center****•****Arctic Initiative at Harvard Kennedy School****•****Alaska Institute for Justice** _Comment submitted by Permafrost Pathways at Woodwell Climate Research Center in response to the_ _National Environmental Policy Act Guidance on Consideration of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Climate Change_ _88 Fr 1196_ This comment on the “National Environmental Policy Act Guidance on Consideration of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Climate Change” (hereinafter “Guidance”) is submitted on behalf of the Permafrost Pathways project at Woodwell Climate Research Center. Woodwell Climate Research Center (“Woodwell Climate”) is a non-profit organization based in Falmouth, Massachusetts that comprises research scientists and policy experts who work with partners worldwide to understand the impacts of and to address climate change on a global scale. Woodwell Climate scientists helped to launch the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change in 1992 and shared the Nobel Prize awarded to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2007. The organization continues to have global impact, bringing together cutting-edge science and translating scientific advances into climate policy solutions. In April 2022, the Arctic program at Woodwell Climate launched [Permafrost Pathways](https://permafrost.woodwellclimate.org/), a new initiative funded through the Audacious Project, that is dedicated to amplifying efforts to collect the best data on Arctic carbon emissions, contextualize this information within global emissions budgets, and transform this science into actionable policy recommendations. Permafrost Pathways represents a collaboration between Woodwell Climate and the Arctic Initiative at Harvard Kennedy School, the Alaska Institute for Justice, the Alaska Native Science Commission, and ten Alaska Native tribes that are experiencing immediate impacts of permafrost thaw. Permafrost is ground that has been continually frozen for at least two consecutive years and often for thousands of years. Permafrost extends across the boreal and tundra biomes and in mountain regions across the globe, underlying roughly 38% of Alaska’s land area.[1](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote1sym) As global temperatures rise, this once-frozen ground is beginning to thaw, creating an increasingly unstable and dangerous environment that presents an immediate and localized risk to Alaska and has the potential to exacerbate climate change on a global scale. While several US federal agencies are actively involved in safeguarding the Arctic from environmental degradation and mitigating adverse effects of climate change,[2](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote2sym) most regulatory frameworks do not account for permafrost thaw. Permafrost Pathways consequently recognizes the importance of accounting for permafrost thaw as part of the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) review process. The NEPA environmental review process is a critical tool for promoting sound decisions that are equitable, cohesive, coordinated, and informed by science. To that end, Permafrost Pathways commends the Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ) for its continued commitment to revitalizing and strengthening NEPA through regulatory revisions and publication of the Guidance. Accounting for permafrost thaw as part of this Guidance, however, is necessary to ensure that federal agencies are implementing NEPA in a manner that effectively safeguards the natural environment within the context of a changing climate. **NEPA reviews of major federal actions in Alaska have not adequately considered immediate and long-term impacts of permafrost thaw.** Among the most observable impacts of permafrost thaw is thaw-induced ground collapse, which can contribute to the destruction of infrastructure, homes, schools, medical facilities, roads, and public utilities that ensure access to electricity, clean water, and other necessities. When compounded with other environmental hazards, such as flooding and erosion, permafrost thaw can cause unparalleled damage to the built environment and cause community-wide displacement.[3](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote3sym) Despite these risks, permafrost thaw has not yet been consistently or effectively integrated into the NEPA review process for projects relating to natural ecosystems or the built environment. The implications of such exclusion on existing projects are readily apparent: for example, permafrost thaw is impacting critical military infrastructure, including at the Northern Warfare Training Center at Fort Wainwright, Alaska;[4](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote4sym) it is also well established that thawing permafrost threatens to destabilize certain sections of the Trans-Alaska Pipeline (TAP), thereby compromising its structural integrity and increasing the risk of a devastating oil spill.[5](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote5sym) Even if the implications of permafrost thaw on these projects were not readily recognized at the time of the NEPA review, NEPA provides that, for significant issues that emerge after an EIS is approved, a Supplemental EIS (SEIS) should be conducted. The best science that is available today includes new information on permafrost thaw, as well as other climate change-induced changes, such as extreme precipitation, fishery collapse, erosion, and warming water temperatures. Recently published SEIS’ for projects previously approved in Alaska do seem to reflect increased recognition of permafrost thaw: For example, the Department of Energy’s Final SEIS for the Alaska Liquefied Natural Gas Project included assessments of permafrost impact, quantification of said impact, and mitigation of further loss.[6](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote6sym) Such inclusion was likely in response to a recommendation from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency that DOE/FERC “[d]evelop and include estimates for each greenhouse gas associated with impacts to permafrost soils (e.g., thaw/degradation) from construction activities proposed under…the Life Cycle Analysis,”[7](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote7sym) and to several public comments calling for similar consideration.[8](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote8sym) In addition, the Final EIS prepared for the Willow Master Development Plan (DOI-BLM-AK-0000-2018-0004-EIS) included an assessment of permafrost impacts as part of the “affected environment and environmental consideration” section and its review of “cumulative effects.”[9](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote9sym) Unfortunately, these projects may have already contributed to irreversible permafrost thaw. The Guidance should help to ensure timely issuance of SEIS’ that adequately consider permafrost thaw. In particular, it should lead the Army Corps of Engineers to conduct an SEIS for the proposed Donlin Gold mine project (as has been requested by federally recognized Tribes in Alaska);[10](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote10sym) and ultimately help agencies to avoid arbitrary and capricious action under NEPA, as is alleged in pending litigation over the Final EIS and Record of Decision for Lease Sale 258 in the Cook Inlet of Alaska.[11](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote11sym) **Estimated GHG emissions from permafrost thaw are “reasonably foreseeable” and should be used to assess present and future impacts, despite current exclusion from most assessments and scenarios.** The Guidance states that agencies generally should quantify reasonably foreseeable direct and indirect gross and net GHG emissions increases or reductions, both for individual pollutants and aggregated in terms of carbon dioxide equivalence by using the best available science and data. To quantify these emissions, the CEQ recommends various resources available on its GHG Accounting Tools website. Most of these tools, however, are unlikely to account for potential GHG emissions from permafrost thaw, as this phenomenon is generally missing from national and international monitoring, modeling, and assessments. Only two of 11 Earth System Models that informed the latest IPCC report (Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6, CMIP6)—which is recognized by the international community as the “best available science and data”— included permafrost;[12](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote12sym) neither of these models represented abrupt thaw, wildfire-mediated thaw, or the release of carbon from below-ground combustion during wildfire.[13](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote13sym) Assessments from the US Global Change Research Program (USGCRP) also lack comprehensive consideration of permafrost thaw and emissions from compounding events. Nevertheless, for almost a decade, the IPCC has reported with high confidence the likelihood of a permafrost carbon feedback on global climate. Experts predict that Arctic permafrost currently stores 1.4 trillion tons of carbon—or at least twice as much carbon as is currently in the Earth’s atmosphere (not accounting for carbon in subsea permafrost).[14](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote14sym) Without aggressive, near-term climate mitigation, the resulting carbon emissions could be as high as 550 Gt CO2 by 2100, on par with or even exceeding continued emissions from the United States based on current rates.[15](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote15sym) Permafrost emissions could therefore take up more than 40% of the remaining carbon budget to limit warming to 1.5°C—the threshold beyond which we expect substantially worse outcomes for permafrost, ice sheets, and coral reefs.[16](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote16sym) The Guidance should explain that scientific uncertainty as to emission levels should not preclude consideration of such emissions as part of the NEPA review process, and should caution agencies about gaps and inconsistencies in existing accounting tools. The Guidance heavily defers to agencies on the “reasonability” of foreseeable GHG emissions, and further advises agencies to “remain aware of the evolving body of scientific information;” as noted, however, the exclusion of permafrost from existing tools and models (including EPA databases and those maintained by CEQ) may create an additional burden on agencies that lack the resources and information to adequately quantify the impact of a major federal action on permafrost thaw. When considering the effects of climate change on a Proposed Action, for instance, the Guidance recognizes as informative the assessments from the USGCRP (and others), yet recent National Climate Assessments have provided only cursory consideration of permafrost thaw as a mitigation and adaptation challenge. **Agencies should remain apprised of the latest research on the potential acceleration of permafrost thaw and its foreseeable impact on GHG emissions.** Ensuring the efficacy of NEPA reviews in accounting for foreseeable GHG emissions and ultimately considering context for such emissions warrants additional consideration from the CEQ. CEQ suggests as a best practice for agencies to “where relevant,…summarize and cite to available scientific literature to help explain the real-world effects—including those that will be experienced locally in relation to the proposed action—associated with an increase in GHG emissions that contribute to climate change, such as sea-level rise, temperature changes, ocean acidity, and more frequent and severe wildfires and drought, and human health effects (including to underserved populations).” Increasingly, scientific literature and the international scientific community is recognizing that certain actions—particularly, protecting permafrost regions and the cryosphere, generally—is a matter of urgent global concern that impacts human communities and natural ecosystems that exist beyond polar and mountain regions. Integrating this analysis as part of a NEPA review, however, is unlikely to occur without more explicit guidance from CEQ. The Guidance should offer more explicit direction and clarify the expectations for disseminating emerging research on permafrost that would inform both a quantitative and qualitative assessment (as is permitted under the Guidance). To ensure that agencies are not unreasonably burdened by the need to stay apprised of the latest assessments, quantification, and modeling of emissions from permafrost thaw, the Guidance could recognize the role of the CEQ in promoting available avenues for knowledge sharing. The Interagency Agency Research Policy Committee (IARPC), for example, remains an underutilized platform through which federal agencies, non-federal researchers, and Arctic communities may engage in knowledge and information exchange relevant to NEPA reviews. The Guidance would also benefit from stressing greater urgency and responsibility of agencies to seriously consider measurable impacts of proposed actions on cumulative GHG emissions and global climate change. **NEPA should advance coordinated adaptation and resilience responses in the Arctic and promote environmental justice for Arctic communities.** Importantly, the Guidance recognizes the disproportionate risks that climate change poses to certain groups, including Tribal Nations and Indigenous communities; it could nevertheless do more to ensure a coordinated, inclusive and equitable approach to NEPA reviews. Alaska Native communities that have contributed the least to climate change are currently pursuing a range of adaptation strategies to respond to permafrost thaw, storm impacts, coastal and riverine erosion, and flooding.[17](https://downloads.regulations.gov/CEQ-2022-0005-0277/attachment_1.docx#sdfootnote17sym) Such strategies include community-led relocation, which can best occur with active involvement and funding from the US federal government. Historically, however, NEPA has not helped to facilitate such action, but has actually hindered these efforts.
---Source URL: https://www.the-innovation.org/article/doi/10.59717/j.xinn-geo.2026.100185
Source title: Global warming driving increased winter CO 2 emissions in the ...
Source type: web
Content:
INTRODUCTION
Permafrost covers approximately 22% of the land area in the Northern Hemisphere (NH),1 predominantly situated in high-latitude (the Arctic) and high-altitude regions (the Tibetan Plateau: TP).2 Although the permafrost areas across the TP are much less compared to the Arctic, the degradation of mountain permafrost has significant impacts on regional and global energy, hydrology, and carbon cycling.3,4 It is particularly noteworthy that these permafrost areas store about 50% of the global soil organic carbon (SOC).5 Global warming accelerates permafrost thaw, enhancing microbial decomposition of SOC, which releases carbon dioxide (CO2) into the atmosphere and creates positive climate feedbacks that intensify climate change.6-8 The potential impact of permafrost carbon feedback on global warming has gained increasing recognition due to its substantial contribution.9-11
The Arctic and TP permafrost regions experience distinct climate change conditions,3 with the Arctic showing a higher rate of temperature and precipitation increase during cold seasons compared to the TP.12,13 Although both regions exhibit amplified warming relative to the global average,14-16 recent winter warming (October to April) has been particularly pronounced.17,18 Rising temperatures and elevated CO2 levels enhance terrestrial carbon uptake during the growing season,19-21 while increasing soil temperature accelerate ecosystem respiration and subsequent CO2 release in winter.22,23 Previous studies suggest that carbon emissions caused by winter warming may offset additional growing season carbon uptake under future climate scenarios.24-28 However, exact quantification of these carbon emissions is still lacking, and the mechanisms driving winter carbon release remain poorly understood. Furthermore, regional disparities in emissions, along with the factors behind these differences, are not fully clear, hindering accurate predictions of carbon-climate feedback in permafrost regions.
Most previous studies have examined the Arctic and TP permafrost independently. Due to differences in soil, vegetation, and climatic conditions, the mechanisms driving winter CO2 emissions in these regions may vary, leading to different sensitivities to warming. Analyzing both regions within a unified framework provides an opportunity for new insights into these mechanisms.3 Winter warming directly affects frozen layer thickness and increases the frequency of freeze-thaw events.29,30 Enhanced freeze-thaw intensity promotes CO2 release during thawing periods by altering soil structure, microbial communities, and nutrient availability.31 Additionally, the deepening of active layer thickness (ALT) will decrease soil moisture, subsequently resulting in a significant increase in the CO2 flux.32 Hence, winter warming strongly influences CO2 emissions by altering soil temperature and water content.33 In fact, previous studies revealed that winter carbon release in permafrost regions depends on various environmental factors such as air temperature, soil hydrothermal conditions, ALT, microbial properties, SOC, vegetation, and snow depth.24,25,34-36 Especially the peatlands widely distributed in the Arctic are more vulnerable to permafrost degradation, accelerating CO2 emissions.37,38 Compared to the Arctic, permafrost in the TP has higher mean annual ground temperatures, deeper ALT, and lower SOC storage.2,39 Additionally, permafrost degradation in the TP has been more rapid than in the Arctic,3,40 but the drivers of winter CO2 release are poorly constrained. Therefore, it is crucial to elucidate the spatiotemporal changes and drivers of winter CO2 emissions by incorporating complex environmental factors across NH permafrost regions while accounting for regional differences. By examining both regions together, we gain deeper insights into the mechanisms regulating winter carbon emissions under varying soil and climate conditions, providing a more comprehensive perspective on permafrost carbon dynamics.
Winter carbon release from permafrost regions represents a significant uncertainty in land carbon-climate feedbacks in the NH. Previous estimates suggest a substantial winter loss of 1,662 Tg C yr−1 in the pan-Arctic permafrost region from 2003 to 2017, with an uncertainty of 813 Tg C yr−1.25 Over the past few decades, winter CO2 release has shown strong temporal variability and spatial heterogeneity.41,42 Although current Earth System Models (ESMs) have improved in simulating biogeochemical processes related to permafrost,43,44 their estimates of winter CO2 emissions often do not align well with in-situ measurements,45-47 partly due to incomplete accounting for regional disparities in mechanisms, especially soil hydrothermal processes.48 Consequently, projections of terrestrial carbon feedback to climate change may lack robustness. Recent advancements in compiling long-term in-situ CO2 flux observations and high-resolution datasets49-51 in the Arctic and TP permafrost regions now make it possible to explore regulatory mechanisms of winter CO2 emissions at a hemispherical scale.
In this study, we collected 2,125 monthly winter flux measurements from 151 sites (1989-2022) in the Arctic and 362 monthly fluxes from 15 sites (2003-2022) in the TP to analyze the patterns, trends, and drivers of winter CO2 emissions across the NH permafrost regions. We first explored the response of winter CO2 emissions to soil hydrothermal conditions. We then separately upscaled winter CO2 flux across the Arctic and TP permafrost regions from 1982 to 2022 using extremely randomized trees (ETR) models, and estimated the annual CO2 budget by integrating outputs of 18 dynamic global vegetation models (DGVMs) from TRENDY project.27,28,52 Furthermore, we examined the spatial-temporal variations of winter CO2 emissions from 1982 to 2022 in the Arctic and TP permafrost regions. Finally, we quantified the contributions of environmental factors to long-term variabilities and trends of winter CO2 emissions using partial correlation analysis and a statistical model.53 The objectives of this study are to gain a comprehensive picture of the trajectory of winter CO2 emissions across the NH permafrost regions and to understand regulating mechanisms of winter CO2 emissions in a unified framework.
Carbon flux data
We synthesized a dataset of in-situ CO2 flux in the NH permafrost regions using eddy covariance, chamber, and snow diffusion methods (Text S1). In total, we used the CO2 flux data at 166 in-situ sites (Figure 1A), resulting in a total of 2,487 monthly winter CO2 flux measurements. There are 151 carbon flux sites in the Arctic permafrost region obtained from ABCflux database,50 FLUXNET2015 dataset (Table S1),49 and published literature.26 While other 15 eddy covariance sites in the TP permafrost region were obtained from publications and unpublished observational data (Table S1). The monthly winter CO2 fluxes were primarily measured using the eddy covariance (51%) and transparent chamber (34%) method, and snow diffusion methods (15%). Critically, chamber-measured CO2 flux data in the Arctic underwent rigorous screening and quality control to ensure accurate quantification of net ecosystem CO2 exchange.50 Further details regarding the data coverage, limitations and uncertainties are described in the ABCflux data description.50 Furthermore, we compared three measurement methods using the method of Li et al. (2022),24 and found no statistically significant differences (Figure S1). Thus, we synthesized the CO2 fluxes data to expand spatial-temporal coverage of observational constraints.53,54 Since we obtained raw high-frequency CO2 flux measurements at five sites in the TP permafrost region, we implemented the quality control, gap-filling, and monthly data aggregation (Text S2). The CO2 flux data were taken from 1989 to 2022, encompassing both the Arctic and TP permafrost zones (Figure 1). Specifically, there were 2,125 monthly fluxes distributed in the Arctic while 362 monthly fluxes distributed in the TP permafrost regions. In this study, we defined the growing season as May to September and the winter as October 1 to next April 30,55 instead of phenological definition based on the beginning of the first 7-day smoothed daily mean air temperature higher or lower than zero.24
Geospatial data
Winter CO2 emissions are affected by a multitude of factors, such as climate, permafrost, and biological activity. Based on a comprehensive review of recent literature,24-25,54-55 we selected ten environmental factors related to climate, vegetation, snow cover, soil, and permafrost for modeling. Climatic factors included monthly average air temperature (TA), monthly precipitation (P) and surface net solar radiation (RD). For soil factors, we considered SOC storage, monthly average soil temperature (ST) and soil moisture (SM) at 3.5-cm depth. Leaf area index (LAI) and snow depth (SD) were used to represent the impact of vegetation and snow cover, respectively. Moreover, we used the active layer thickness (ALT) and permafrost type (PermaType) as permafrost factors.
Considering the spatial and temporal resolution required for upscaling winter CO2 flux, we obtained monthly data on TA, P, RD, LAI, SD, ST, and SM with a spatial resolution of 0.1° from the advanced ERA5-Land reanalysis product,56 which provides high accuracy and physical consistency across variables. Specifically, ST and SM were extracted from “soil temperature level 1” and “volumetric soil water layer 1” datasets in ERA5-Land reanalysis product, respectively. To ensure comprehensive spatiotemporal coverage, LAI data were from outputs of multiple ESMs in the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) (Text S3).57,58 The ALT data with a spatial resolution of 0.5° were obtained from a previously published dataset40,59 based on the modified Stefan equation, which shows a strong correlation with observed values. Surface SOC storage data (top 30 cm) with a spatial resolution of 1 km were retrieved from published literature,60,61 which has been validated for applicability in the Arctic and TP permafrost regions.
Additionally, we further divided the carbon flux sites into peatlands and non-peatlands, with classification data sourced from Global Peatland Map 2.0.62 The vegetation type was derived from the European Space Agency Climate Change Initiative's (ESA CCI) land cover product to ensure comparability with the ABCflux.50
Observational data analyses
We analyzed the discrepancies in winter CO2 emissions between the Arctic and TP permafrost regions, different permafrost zones (continuous, discontinuous, and sporadic/isolated), peatland and non-peatland, and different ecosystem types (forest, shrub, grassland, wetland, and tundra) using the in-situ CO2 flux data. Before statistical analysis, we examined the normality and variance homogeneity of the in-situ winter CO2 fluxes. Then, the non-parametric Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (Figures 1B-D) and Kruskal-Wallis H-test (Figure 1E) were employed to scrutinize the dissimilarity in winter CO2 emissions among them.24
To assess long-term annual trends in winter CO2 emissions across the Arctic permafrost regions in comparison with the TP, we chose a linear mixed effect model to detect changes.26,41 To mitigate the impact of temporal disparities in observational data coverage, we analyzed winter CO2 emission trends during the shared observational period (2003–2022). We fit separately for the Arctic and the TP according to the following steps. Firstly, we excluded the site years with missing winter months (that is, all 7 months were required for inclusion). Then, winter CO2 flux in the compiled dataset served as dependent variable in a linear mixed effect model, where measurement year acted the fixed effect and random slopes and intercepts for each site. Meanwhile, an autoregressive (AR1) correlation structure was added to the model to account for temporal autocorrelation within sites. Furthermore, given the increasing number of observational sites since the beginning of our time series, we re-ran our model using the complete Arctic dataset to assess the robustness of our findings against potential temporal biases in data collection (Figure S2).26
To elucidate the response of winter CO2 emissions to soil hydrothermal conditions change, we analyzed its nonlinear relationship with soil temperature and soil moisture in the Arctic and TP permafrost regions, respectively. We used in-situ monitored topsoil temperature to obtain more reliable results, despite the risk of overestimation. Since negative winter CO2 fluxes may arise from low-flux conditions, and to reduce the interference of heterogeneity among ecosystems and minimize the contribution from autotrophic respiration (Ra), we excluded winter CO2 flux with value less than 0 and soil temperature greater than 2°C before conducting the fitting to maintain consistency.25 Then, we used the Van’t Hoff equation51,63 to explore the relationship between winter CO2 emissions and topsoil temperature, and calculated the temperature sensitivity (apparent Q10) of winter CO2 emissions. i.e., y=aebt (1) and Q10=e10b (2). Meanwhile, the 95% confidence interval for the fitted parameters was calculated using the output from 1,000 bootstrap samples.
We analyzed the response of winter CO2 emissions to soil moisture to examine soil moisture threshold for the Arctic and TP permafrost regions,64 respectively. To enhance the reliability of our findings, we established the response relationship of winter CO2 emissions to soil moisture using in-situ observational data, specifically 645 months (30%) of the Arctic records and 304 months (84%) of the TP records. Firstly, we fitted linear and nonlinear (general additive models) regressions for the winter CO2 flux versus soil moisture data, and selected the model with the lowest Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) value, because a threshold only occurs when the nonlinear regression fits the data better than the linear regression (Table S2). Then, we fitted their relationship using threshold regression model,65 which provides four types of threshold effects: step (discontinuous models), hinge (continuous models), segmented (continuous models) and ‘stegmented’ (discontinuous models). In this study, we chose segmented threshold model that achieved the lowest AIC value. The model provides a parameter for describing the value for soil moisture in the predictor that shows the shift in winter CO2 flux (regression slope and P for segmented). Additionally, to further test whether the identified thresholds significantly affected the slope of the fitted lines, we bootstrapped 500 times the linear regressions at both sides of each threshold for soil moisture and tested significance using a Wilcoxon test (Figure S3).
To quantify the impact of environmental variables on winter CO2 emissions, we categorized them into five groups: climate (TA, P, RD), vegetation (LAI), snow cover (SD), soil (SM, ST, SOC), and permafrost (ALT). We applied Z-score normalization to all environmental and dependent variable data before performing hierarchical partitioning.66 This approach allowed us to examine the relative contributions of different environmental factors to winter CO2 emissions separately for the Arctic and TP permafrost regions, mitigating the influence of variable interactions (Figure S4).
The “mgcv” package was used to fit general additive models, while “chngpt” package was used to fit segmented regressions. The hierarchical partitioning was performed using the “rdacca.hp” package. These analyses were implemented in R 4.3.1.
Winter CO2 flux upscaling
Given the spatial and temporal heterogeneity in winter CO2 emissions and environmental factors, we employed random forest (RF), ETR, and gradient boosting regression trees (GBRT) models to separately simulate winter CO2 flux in the Arctic and TP permafrost regions by integrating climate, vegetation, snow cover, soil, and permafrost factors. Meanwhile, to ensure high model reliability, we incorporated extensive in-situ observational environmental data and supplemented missing environmental variables with geospatial datasets. Since tree-based ensemble models such as RF, ETR, and GBRT are inherently insensitive to feature scales, we did not normalize data during model training, testing, and prediction. Furthermore, we applied one-hot encoding to permafrost type as a categorical variable, converting it into numerical inputs for the machine learning algorithms.
First, all winter CO2 fluxes and environmental variables were randomly split into an 80:20 ratio for model training and testing, respectively. Second, we optimized model hyperparameters—including n_estimators (number of trees), max_features (number of features considered for each split), and max_depth (maximum tree depth)—using random search and ten-fold cross-validation. Model accuracy was mainly assessed using root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and the coefficient of determination (R2), where larger RMSE and MAE values indicate greater errors, while higher R2 values indicate better model performance. Additionally, model performance was also evaluated using other auxiliary metrics, including best score, mean squared error, and Pearson correlation coefficient. The performances of the models in the Arctic and TP are shown in Figures S5 - S6. The test results indicate that the ETR model outperformed the others in both the Arctic (MAE = 3.94 g C m−2 month−1, RMSE = 5.67 g C m−2 month−1, R2 = 0.67) and the TP (MAE = 2.76 g C m−2 month−1, RMSE = 3.76 g C m−2 month−1, R2 = 0.82). These results are consistently validated across auxiliary evaluation metrics, confirming ETR's superior performance (Table S3). Previous studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of ETR in the spatial upscaling of CO2 flux in high-latitude and high-altitude areas.67 Moreover, our model evaluation metrics demonstrated superior performance compared to previous permafrost upscaling efforts.24,25,54
Therefore, using region-specific optimal models, we separately upscaled monthly winter CO2 flux in the Arctic and the TP permafrost regions from October 1982 to April 2023. Firstly, all geospatial data were unified to the same temporal (monthly) and spatial resolution (0.1°) before the upscaling of CO2 flux to ensure final upscaled dataset had the same spatial-temporal resolution as the input variables. Then, for each month, we integrated the raster files of corresponding environmental variables and estimated the monthly CO2 flux of the Arctic and the TP permafrost regions on a per-pixel basis. Furthermore, we assessed the uncertainty by generating 100 bootstrapped model training datasets and using developed 100 individual models. Specifically, we maintained identical model hyperparameters and tuning methods as the model configuration for each iteration, but generated distinct 80% training datasets through randomized seeding. This method enables controlled decoupling of algorithm performance from data perturbations, thereby allowing rigorous assessment of model robustness under input data distribution shifts. Based on these 100 predictions, we aggregated monthly CO2 fluxes into annual winter CO2 emissions for each iteration. We then calculated the standard deviation (s.d.) across iterations for each winter during 1982-2022 to quantify interannual variability uncertainty. Finally, we computed pixel-level statistics during 2013-2022, deriving the mean, s.d., and the coefficient of variation to quantify spatial uncertainty.68 The analysis reveals high uncertainty in the regions with sparse observations, such as the TP, northern Canada, and Mongolia (Figure S7).
All the model computations were conducted using the Scikit-learn package in Python programming language 3.11.5.
Comparison of ETR estimates with DGVMs
We compared our estimated winter CO2 flux in the Arctic and TP permafrost regions with the winter CO2 flux estimated by 18 DGVMs from TRENDY project version 12.27,28 The 18 models are CABLE-POP, CLASSIC, CLM5.0, DLEM, E3SM, EDv3, IBIS, ISAM, ISBA-CTRIP, JSBACH, JULES, LPJml, LPJwsl, LPX-Bern, OCN, ORCHIDEE, SDGVM, and VISIT, which have a monthly output covering the period from 1982 to 2022 across the NH permafrost regions. Based on mechanistic representations of plant physiology, ecology, and biogeochemical processes, these models can simulate key carbon cycle fluxes, such as gross primary production (GPP), Ra, and heterotrophic respiration (Rh), which are usually used for carbon budget assessments in the NH permafrost regions.69 Complete model specifications are documented in Friedlingstein et al. (2023)27. The TRENDY protocol simulations were classified into four scenarios (S0, S1, S2, and S3). DGVMs in S3 are forced by varying CO2, climate change, and land cover change and models with N cycle have time-varying N inputs. Since the DGVMs do not directly provide the net carbon fluxes, we calculated winter CO2 flux using the outputs of GPP, Ra and Rh in simulation S3. For January to April 2023, we filled data gaps using corresponding monthly data from 2022. To reduce uncertainties, we employed the multi-model ensemble mean for analysis — a strategy demonstrating higher reliability than individual models.52,70 Then, we compared the ETR estimates with the net carbon fluxes from the TRENDY in simulation S3. Additionally, we computed the net CO2 uptake during the growing season for the NH, Arctic and TP permafrost regions using the 18-model ensemble. Finally, we derived the annual CO2 budget for these regions by combining the winter CO2 flux estimated by the ETR with growing season CO2 flux estimated by TRENDY.
Spatial and temporal changes from 1982 to 2022
Given the interannual fluctuations in winter CO2 emissions, we considered the annual average of the past 10 years (2013-2022) as a representative estimate of current winter CO2 emissions. To quantify the change of current winter CO2 emissions in the Arctic and the TP permafrost regions relative to the past 41 years, we calculated the anomalies between the upscaling result for current and the period 1982-2022 mean. Then, we divided the past 41 years into two subperiods, 1982-2000 and 2001-2022, and calculated their anomalies. Additionally, to examine the spatial and temporal changes of winter CO2 emissions of the NH permafrost regions in the past 41 years, we analyzed the trend of winter CO2 flux for each grid cell of the permafrost regions during the periods of 1982-2022, 1982-2000, and 2001-2022, respectively. The slope of linear least-squares regression was used to describe trend over time. Moreover, since the start of the growing season (SOS) first occurs on the eastern edge of the TP in early May and progresses westward, reaching the western edge in late June,71 we recalculated the results using both October–March and November–March as winter definitions to minimize the influence of an earlier SOS, confirming the findings remain unaffected (Figure S17). Finally, we calculated the proportion of pixel values greater than 0 and less than 0 for these raster files. When the value of the pixel was greater than zero, the winter CO2 emissions was considered to exhibit a positive trend, while when it was less than 0, it was considered a negative trend. Finally, we used the same method to calculate the winter CO2 emissions trends using DGVMs from TRENDY project over different time periods and compared the results.
Quantifying dominant controls on winter CO2 emissions variability and trend
We used partial correlation analysis to determine the dominant controls on interannual winter CO2 emission variations from 2001 to 2022 in NH permafrost regions.53 This method statistically removes the influence of other controlling variables, allowing us to assess the effect of each environmental factor individually.72 We conducted the analysis separately for the Arctic and TP on a per-pixel basis. Specifically, we calculated the annual anomaly of each variable by subtracting the 2001–2022 mean and removing the interannual trend, then computed partial correlation coefficients between each environmental factor and winter CO2 emissions. For each grid cell, the driver with the highest absolute value of partial correlation coefficient was determined as the dominant driver. Finally, we calculated the area coverage of each dominant factor for all grid cells in the NH, Arctic, and TP permafrost regions.
We further used a statistical linear regression model to quantify the influence of different environmental drivers on interannual variability and trends in winter CO2 emissions (2001–2022).53 We built a baseline model (ModelAll) using all temporally variable input factors, then conducted eight controlled experiments for the Arctic and TP (ModelP, ModelTA, ModelST, ModelSM, ModelRD, ModelLAI, ModelSD, ModelALT).
